skill.name: systematic-debugging
skill.description: A structured debugging workflow for finding root causes before applying fixes. It guides reproduction, tracing, pattern comparison, hypothesis testing, and verification.
skill.summary: Find root causes before changing code.
skill.author: Asmayaseen

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command-execution patterns, plus weak-crypto and reconnaissance flags. Review showed the command patterns are markdown debugging examples, and the other high-risk flags are false positives. No evidence found of malicious intent, prompt injection, network access, secret access, or automatic command execution.

low_findings.title: Documentation Contains External Command Examples
low_findings.description: The static external-command findings are true patterns in markdown examples, not executable skill code. They show git initialization, subprocess use, and a bash test loop for root cause tracing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged commands appear inside fenced markdown examples that explain debugging techniques. No evidence found that the skill runs these commands automatically.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Flags Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-crypto flags point to descriptive text and stack-trace guidance, not cryptographic code. The reconnaissance flags point to ordinary debugging guidance about unclear data origins and component boundaries.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain no cryptographic algorithm, credential handling, or system enumeration request. They are plain debugging instructions in the skill documentation.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Patterns in Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Markdown examples include subprocess.run, an exec_file git command, and shell command substitution. These are useful debugging examples but should be reviewed before use in a real repository.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command patterns are present and could affect a user environment if copied. The confidence is lower for security impact because they are examples, not active code.

content.user_title: Debug Issues with Root Cause Tracing
content.value_statement: Random fixes can hide the real cause and create regressions. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a disciplined workflow for tracing, testing, and verifying fixes.
actual_capabilities: Guides root cause investigation before proposing code changes.
Defines a four-phase process for tracing, comparison, testing, and implementation.
Helps compare failing behavior with working examples in the same codebase.
Encourages small hypothesis tests instead of stacked fixes.
Includes guidance for stack traces and test pollution investigation.
Promotes defense-in-depth checks after a bug is fixed.
limitations: It does not diagnose a bug without access to project evidence.
It does not replace language-specific debuggers or profiling tools.
Some examples include commands that users should review before running.
It is less useful for exploratory code reading without a concrete issue.

use_cases.title: Fix a Failing Test
use_cases.description: Trace the failure from the visible symptom back to the original input or setup problem.

use_cases.title: Investigate CI-Only Bugs
use_cases.description: Compare local and CI behavior across configuration, environment, and component boundaries.

use_cases.title: Review Repeated Fix Attempts
use_cases.description: Pause after several failed fixes and decide whether the architecture or assumptions need review.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Debugging Session
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner: use when an error appears and you need a structured first pass.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use systematic debugging on this error. First list the evidence we have, then identify what must be reproduced before any fix.

prompt_templates.title: Trace a Root Cause
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate: use when the failing line is probably only a symptom.
prompt_templates.prompt: Trace this failure backward through the call chain. Show each caller, the data passed, and the earliest suspicious source.

prompt_templates.title: Test a Hypothesis
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced: use when you have one likely cause and need a minimal proof.
prompt_templates.prompt: Form one root-cause hypothesis for this bug. Design the smallest test or instrumentation change that can confirm or reject it.

prompt_templates.title: Recover from Failed Fixes
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced: use after several attempted fixes did not solve the issue.
prompt_templates.prompt: We tried multiple fixes and the bug remains. Reassess the architecture, identify hidden coupling, and propose the next investigation step.

output_examples.input: A test creates files in the source directory instead of a temporary directory.
output: Symptom: files appear in the wrong directory during tests.
Immediate cause: a workspace operation receives an empty directory value.
Root cause path: test setup exposes a value before initialization completes.
Next step: add a failing test and validate directory inputs at the source.

output_examples.input: A fix changed three files, but the original failure still happens.
output: Stop adding fixes and return to evidence gathering.
Separate each change from the observed behavior it was meant to test.
Keep one hypothesis and verify it with the smallest possible change.

output_examples.input: A CI failure cannot be reproduced locally.
output: Compare environment, configuration, dependency versions, and command inputs.
Log data at each component boundary once, then analyze where values diverge.
Avoid changing production logic until the divergence is identified.

best_practices: Reproduce the issue before proposing a fix.
Trace bad data to its source, not only to the line that failed.
Test one hypothesis with one minimal change at a time.
anti_patterns: Applying a quick patch before reading the full error and stack trace.
Stacking several changes before checking whether any one change helped.
Continuing repeated fixes after evidence suggests a larger design issue.

faq.question: When should I use this skill?
faq.answer: Use it when a bug, failed test, or unexpected behavior needs diagnosis before code changes.

faq.question: Does it run commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides debugging guidance and examples. Users should approve any command before running it.

faq.question: Can it help with flaky tests?
faq.answer: Yes. It encourages condition-based waiting and evidence collection instead of timing guesses.

faq.question: Is it only for Python projects?
faq.answer: No. The process is language independent, although some examples use Python and shell syntax.

faq.question: What makes it different from normal troubleshooting?
faq.answer: It requires root cause evidence before fixes and limits changes to one tested hypothesis.

faq.question: What should happen after three failed fixes?
faq.answer: Stop and reassess assumptions, coupling, and architecture before making more changes.
